speaker: interviewer
text: Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're almost finished. After this tape, we're finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Down to the end or after--

speaker: interviewer
text: One more tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the war? Or just to the end?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eleanor?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, hold on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bart?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Eleanor there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's keep--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4. My name is Debbi Portnoy. Today's date is May 18, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Mr. Nathan Sobel.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I came in, and I hid in this-- another little hidden place that I could-- I could be hidden and  not seen. When you come in, you would think it's a-- it's a cellar. And then right after me, a few guys with the guns came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the Poles, the AK, Armija Krajowa. And they said, who are you? Where is the little one? He said, well, you killed my wife and everybody a week ago. And he said, where is the little one? He says, he went for water. He didn't come back. So they ran out. And he said, please close the door. They can see it. And I closed the door quickly. And I had an axe next to me, standing. And right after they came in, they started tormenting and torturing this guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 9:00

speaker: interviewee
text: 00 in the morning. This was going on until about 5 o'clock. What they didn't do to him. They promised they'll take him to a hospital. They'll take him here, there. He said, they only must find me because they must-- they're going to cut me into pieces if they don't catch me. He says, he cheated us already three times. We must catch him and take revenge. They said, you tell us where he is, and we're going to take you to the hospital. And we're going to heal you. And we're going to do this and this. Anyway, around 4 or 5 o'clock in the afternoon, he says, I don't know where he went. He went for water, and he disappeared. I don't know where he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one guy came in, and he says-- he cursed him out really badly. And he says, those, those Jews, they suck our blood, God damn it. And he took two bullets, boom, and another bullet went into me in the other side. But they didn't hit me. It was-- just bypassed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they locked the thing. And they-- and they ran out. I didn't know what happened. I thought, he's still alive. But they locked the door from the outside. In the middle of the night. I woke up and said, oh, I got to give you water. I've got to give him the rope because I couldn't lift him up. At one point, I tried to commit suicide. But the knife fell out. And I said, no. I'm not going to commit suicide. And I touched Iol. And he was all a-- frozen body. He was very, very cold. He was all frozen, ice. And so I knew I had to go-- run-- because they'll come the next day again. They were-- they were looking for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked out in this cold winter night. Trees were, like, crying. Everything was like-- at that point, I realized I thought I was the, the last Jew alive in this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this happened for several months. I kept wandering. I didn't care really what happens to me. They tried to shoot after me, and this and that. And I wandered through areas where the partisans were. I figured, maybe I'll join in with some partisans. Maybe they'll take me in. They wouldn't kill me. But even the partisans were killing Jews. Ukrainians were killing Jews. There were Polish Underground that were killing Jews. Everybody is killing Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'd figure I'll go no matter what will happen. And so it was. I wandered to some villages, and I presented myself. And I said who I am and what I am. And I says, you want to do with me whatever you wanted to do with me. I'm a little boy. He said, what can you do? I said, well, I can-- I can tend to the cattle. I can make shoes. I can make clothes. You can make clothes? I said, yeah. So they took me in. And all of a sudden, they start fighting over me. And I would cut for women-- to making rough cloth. I was making shirts and dresses, pants. I would cut the shape of shoes from leather, and I would make shoes for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, I became a very valuable commodity. And they would make a list who gets me next.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would skin a calf. I said, kill, I can't. I can't look at it. But if you do it, I'll skin it, and the same with the pig. He says, you've been doing it long? I said, oh, all my life I've been skinning pigs.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is what I did. Whatever I had to do, I did. And it was going on until around May or June. At one point, I was tending to cattle in the field. And I, I was running. At one point, I had to run across a main road. And I knew that the Russians were coming. The Germans were running there, and they were hunting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew that the yahrzeit is coming up, the first yahrzeit. And I had to get a candle to light to say Kaddish. And so I'm running from the Germans. And where I'm going to get the candle to say yahrzeit, to light the candle? And where I'm going to get, get the wax? And where I'm going to get fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I'm running across the main road where the trains are running here. And they're running there. And I'm running across this. And I find a piece of tallow, as if it came down from heaven. I grabbed it and I ran. And I styled a candle-- with a piece of thread from my szmatas, made a candle. Then I took two stones and made a fire. And I found a, a tree that was hollowed out from thunder or lightning. And I put a candle in there. And I laid the fire. And I said Kaddish, whatever I remembered. And I remember lighting this candle. And I was hiding in the forest a whole night. And the next morning I see big masses of people, like thousands and thousands and marching, marching. Finally, I didn't know what it was. And nobody is saying nothing. They're not shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I see people standing. So I inched over and said, who are these? They said, oh, they're the Red ones. It was the Red Army came in. It was June 19, 1944. When I found-- they said, oh, they liberated us. I said, they liberated what? What did we liberate-- from what? Where, where are we going? Are we going home? Where are we going? So finally, I ran into the field. I was so tired from all this. And I fell asleep. I don't know what happened. But I must have stayed a day or a day and a half in that field.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I came out-- that-- that's when another Jewish boy found out that I was alive. He came over. And I met the first Jew. So I was not the, the last Jew. And together, we went to town. The town was totally dead. Finally, at one point, I, I tried to go into my house. And the Ukrainian woman grabbed the neck.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, I'll chop his head off. [SPEAKING UKRAINIAN], she says. I'll chop his head off! He survived? We're going to kill them all! This is already under the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally-- I stayed there for a little while because I met some other few survivors. Altogether out of about 10,000, maybe about 50 Jews survived. And half of them were abnormal already-- mental  and emotional, psychologically and physically. And we stayed for another year or so, a year and a half. And we saw that this is not our place. People started running to the west to the-- through, through Poland, via Poland to Germany where the American forces were. And at one point--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get to a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was-- I was in a DP camp later. But at one point, I wanted to get out. I'm cutting short everything. But I wanted to take revenge.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the Russians, and I told them that I know that whenever we told them they killed Jews, they didn't do a thing. So I developed a plan. I didn't tell anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said that when the Russians-- when the-- when the Germans attacked, they killed Russian women, the, the wives of the officers of the commanders and their children. I said, also, when they started killing the Soviet citizens of the Jewish population, they also killed them. We were also always an 'also.' And I would give them the names. And they would take them off because they killed Russians. And I did a whole bunch of them. I did for, for several months. I didn't tell nobody what I did. And he gave me a name, [RUSSIAN]. [RUSSIAN], in Russian, means little one. And so he called me [RUSSIAN]. He says, you don't have to come. You only have to say, [RUSSIAN], and  they'll know. They'll let you in. Nobody could get into these quarters. Only I could get it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, at one point, I came. He says, I have very good news for you. I said, what is the news? He said, I'm going to send you to a, a Suvorov school. Suvorov school in Moscow is a very important school for major officers and generals and spies.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the first time I'm telling you on tape. I'm going to send you there. You are very good for that. And so they gave  you very good recommendations. And they accepted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of saying thank you and then running out, I says, I'm not going to Russia. He said, where are you going? I said, I'm going to the West. Why are you going to the West?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm not staying here. The earth was full of blood. I have nothing here. I have nobody here. The only one I have-- an aunt in, in Palestine. And that's all I'm going to. I didn't want to say America. America was treif at that time. And as I said this, he rang a bell. And five or six guys came in with their guns. And they're pointing them at me. And he took off all the papers and ran out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I knew, he came back. And he saw that I was the enemy all of a sudden. And I said, Nathan, why, why are you so stupid? Why do you have to tell him the truth? He says, you survived for that? And I figured this is the end. Before the end of the day, I'll be dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, he came back. And I said, look-- and he says, oh, you're stupid. You're a kid. You're a child. You don't know. You don't understand why, da, da, da, da, da. And I said, look, I have nobody. I am not political. I have an aunt. I have  only one aunt in Palestine. I don't even know her name, I said. Her name is Malke Sojbel-- I knew. But I said, I don't even know her name. I want to go to-- I want somebody from my family. I have nobody, I said. I lost everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept begging and pleading with him, and all this. In the end, I saw that he says, you know what? I'll let you go. He said, just write me more if you have any more names. And so I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I took off. I took some people who signed off to my document-- I added on more. And I was able to save them. And I went via Poland. And the Poles were chasing me through Poland. And then I-- they smuggled me across from Poland to Berlin. And in Berlin, the-- they-- I saw they were taking the orphans-- little orphans. We were-- I was one of 43 children. They took us. They're going to ask us if we want to go to America, England, or Palestine. At one point, I wandered off into the Russian zone-- and since I had the Russian Army uniform, which I bought it for a bottle of vodka. It was oversized. It had two left shoes. One was black. One was brown. And the black one had a bullet hole through it. And he grabbed me. He wanted to put me on the truck and send me back to Siberia. And I kept bargaining back and forth. I made myself stupid, dumb, and idiotic.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, no. I'm German. [SPEAKING GERMAN]? I tried to speak in German. I said, I don't understand what he says in Russian, even though I understood very well. And he tried for two hours to get me on this truck. So an officer came over and said, what's going on with you? He says, he's a deserter from the Russian Army. And he doesn't da, da, da, da.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took away whatever I had, which was not much. And the officer, either he was Jewish or he understood. He saw that I couldn't be a soldier with these two left shoes, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, don't you see? He's a stupid kid. He's an idiot. Throw him out! He says, get him out. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]! So he says, OK. Get the hell out of here. So he threw me out through the Brandenburg Gate, where the other side was the American, British, and French zones. So as I was-- crossed to the others zones, I went to the Russian, like this. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- he grabbed the gun and wanted to shoot me. But anyway, within a, a day, I went, went out with 43 children. They took us to Palestine. At that point, they put us in there. They took us to an orphanage home, and the-- one of the-- it was a Jewish organization of women came to visit us and take us on. The woman who came to me, she says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Children, you're going to be in Palestine. Give, give regards to my-- to my husband. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, who is your husband? She says, you don't know? It's David Ben-Gurion. It was the-- Ben-Gurion's wife is the one who took me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stood for about a couple of months in an orphanage home in France, Maison d'Enfants [FRENCH]. And then from there, they took us to, to Marseilles. And in Marseilles-- I'm making it very short-- they took us to, [SIGHS] to the ship. And we have to smuggle on the ship the 43 children, which we had one certificate. We had to smuggle 36 illegal immigrants on top of that. And we, we took the children. We hid them behind the beds and in hiding places, and so on. And we got them all on the ship. Only after two, three days, they realized that the 43 children are eating for 86 people. And we grabbed-- we had a rule. Anything solid goes in your pocket. Any little liquid you eat or drink. If you brought a piece of bread, a potato, an apple, a fruit, you would bring it back and give it to those illegal immigrants. Anyway, we smuggled, and we ended up trying to take them off-- take us off in Alexandria. We tried to jump into the water. Then they brought us to Haifa. Then we tried again to jump into the water unless they take off the illegal immigrants first. And then, when they took them off the ship, then we were able to go. And they distributed us among different kibbutzim. I was in Israel for about six, seven years. And I was in a kibbutz, in a moshav. Then I was working private. I was very weak after the war. Then I discovered-- I found my family in America. And I was-- they wouldn't even take me to the army in Israel. That's how weak I was. I was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means not eligible for the army. And in 1952, I, I got papers to go with a visa-- after waiting for six years, seven years-- to come to America, which I had a grandmother and two aunts and two uncles and a cousin. That's why I came here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and when I came here, after the second day, I went to Brooklyn College. And I was going to go to, to college. They said, do you know any English? I says, well, a little bit. I failed the exam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they let me take English at night. Then they let me take some drafting or whatever. And I finished Brooklyn College at night. And I traveled to the-- went-- a year and a half, I was in Tallahassee, Florida working on the Florida turnpike. I was in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania. I was working different engineering firms. Meanwhile, I finished college.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and when I was graduating, the dean says he's not going to let me graduate in front of 10,000 people because I don't have my high school diploma. He says, Mr. Sobel, where's your high school diploma? This is in the presence-- said it front of all these people. Mr. Sobel, here's your high school diploma and college diploma. Congratulations, lots of luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: That same day, I went to Columbia University. And I finished Colombia with my masters and an honorary PhD--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in urban planning. The-- I graduated Brooklyn College in '59. And in 1962, I graduated Columbia. And I started working pretty seriously-- a city planner. I was later on named to director for urban renewal. I put eight books, including my thesis, on planning and housing. I put out a two-volume atlas of all the urban renewal projects in the City of New York. I had a number of inventions and patents and copyrights and trademarks. You name it, I've had it. And I was married. I have two sons, Andrew and Barton. Barton is here. I think Andrew's also going to come. And my current wife is Eleanor. And she's here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the organization that you started.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started a lot of organizations. I was-- one organization, it was in 1967. The Israeli government came. They said we wanted to earn some money for Israel and also do some publicity. So we formed a group called AINA, American Israel Numismatic Association, which was a nonprofit group, which we-- it was a hobby of collecting coins and medals. And they said, you'll be the editor. I says, I've done a lot of things, but editing, I've not done. He said, you go home. Create a magazine, a journal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went home, and I named it And I named the organization, AINA, American Israel Numismatic Association. It's still in existence. It is the 27th year. And I was the founding editor for several years. And then I was also designer. I'm still on the board of directors of AINA.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I designed about a hundred medals in my life. One of these metals was a peace medal, which includes all the names for peace from all over the world. I did the research, and I designed this metal. One is universal peace. The other side is Middle East peace, which includes a face which looks like patriarch Abraham and the eyes form two sons, Ishmael and Isaac.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about NAJCHS.

speaker: interviewee
text: NAJCHS was created about 10 years ago. I named it also. It's called National Association of Jewish Child Holocaust Survivors. And it's people who were children during the war. And it includes people from Russia to Romania and Poland and, and Germany and France and Italy and Holland-- all the countries in Europe. And we meet once a month, and we meet nationally now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at one point, I was the founding president of this organization. And then at one point, I-- it was bothering how the Holocaust would be remembered. And I created-- and I came up with an idea to call it Shoahaggadah, which every year will be read at 27 day in Nisan.

speaker: interviewee
text: It has nothing to do with the Exodus, but it has to do the job for the Holocaust what the Haggadah did for the Exodus, since only this way will it be remembered. There are thousands of books sitting on the shelves, but people are not reading them. This Shoahaggadah, if you'll have to, to, to read it every year, then you'll remember. And it's a mosaic of different events that took place during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you want to give your children and the world 50 years after the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the world did not, not do well with us. And we did not-- they were not recognized at all during the, the war and after the war. We were just treated as refugees or greenie, or whatever. I don't owe anything to the world at all. And the deniers, who are coming now, while we are still alive, and denying what has happened, while we still bear the wounds, is also very humiliating and insulting to us survivors. And I don't owe anything to anybody in this world. And I'll never forgive. And I'll never forget. It they want to get forgiveness, they'll have to go to the dead people to do [NON-ENGLISH] to ask for forgiveness. A message to the-- to the world itself, to the Jewish world, is to bear witness, [NON-ENGLISH]. You must bear witness to tell what has happened and to remember-- as a whole, we must remember never to forget. And Jewish education is very important to have the education. Otherwise, they'll just go off. Jewish continuity is very important. And then, last but not least, the state of Israel. They have to have a connection to it. Otherwise, the Jewish people, are going-- God forbid, could go under.

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of myself and the Shoah Visual History Foundation, I'd like to thank you. And now we'll take a look at some of your pictures and artifacts and your family members.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture I obtained not long ago. This picture was given to me by my uncle, which is my mother's youngest brother, which he died last year. It was brought to me from Los Angeles. In this picture is my grandfather Nutel Faigen, who, who I'm named after, and my grandmother Hinde Faigen, who, after she was widowed, came to America in the 1920s. And my mother, Bashie Sojbel, and my uncle Zelig Faigen, which ended up in Argentina.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe this picture was taken around 1918 or 1920 in Luboml. It was right after World War I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a family portrait. It was taken around 1935, '36, I believe. It's a family portrait, as I said. The upper row is my mother, to the left Bashie Sojbel, and my father, Avram or Avrum Sojbel. Then is my sister Sara Tova Sojbel. Then is my brother Aaron Tzvi to the right. Then below him is-- to the left is my youngest brother Bentzion Sojbel. And the lowest to the right is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are these letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a bundle of letters which I obtained from my grandmother, aunt, and my uncle from Argentina. These are letters written by my mother to her mother, my grandmother, her sister and brother, my aunt and uncle, and brother in Argentina. It was written before the war, World War II, and during the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about these manuscripts.

speaker: interviewee
text: These manuscripts I obtained last year when my uncle Zelig Faigen-- my mother's youngest brother's wife came to New York. And she brought me those manuscripts. And he bestowed them to me. He wanted me to have them. These are manuscripts that my uncle, a writer and researcher, has done in Luboml, and he continued on doing until he reached Argentina. They would not let him into America. He wouldn't get a visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he ended up going through Argentina as a farmer. That's the only way he could obtain an entry to Argentina. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is of myself, the very first picture after the war. I was in the-- among the group of 43 children. I was in Marseilles. It was in 1945, towards fall of '45. It was-- the suit and all the clothes were given to me by UNRRA, the United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration. On the day of auspices, I traveled to Palestine, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: --probably the only yizkor book. Tell us about this book.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a memorial book, a yizkor book from Luboml, from my town. It was totally translated from Hebrew and Yiddish into English. I did this work. I just completed it. And it's going to go into publication very soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this book.

speaker: interviewee
text: This book I obtained yesterday. It is a-- an exhibition plan for a-- for an exhibition that's going to be done about Luboml Libivne. It's, it's going to be called And a friend of mine-- we went to school together, Aaron Ziegelman-- is underwriting this project, which will show-- will be a, a vivid exhibition of our shtetl, with names, places, and events-- everything will be-- will be on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the early 1930s. It's of my brothers to the left, Aaron Tzvi, then my sister Sara Tova, and then it's myself. It was a picture taken in a studio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this appearing? Where are we seeing this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are we going to see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is going to be part of the exhibition, which we have close to a thousand photographs and objects and relics and mementos from that town. It's going to be a traveling exhibition. The curator is Fred Wasserman, which was the curator for the Ellis Island-- Ellis Island Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this picture part of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is, is part of that book, It's on the second page of that book. It just came out yesterday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is also part of the book. It's the very last page of the book. These are-- we are three friends from Luboml, all survivors, all surviving children. To the left is Moishe Blumin, which lives now in Kibbutz ReShafim in Israel. In the middle is me and to the right is Abraham Getman, which lives now in Long Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken right after our arrival to-- into Israel. It must have been '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this pamphlet.

speaker: interviewee
text: This pamphlet is the, the official journal of the American Israel Numismatic Association, called AINA. I was the one who created this and named it and shaped it. And I was the founding editor for several years. And this organization is the official nonprofit organization, AINA, which deals with Israel's coins, medals, and tokens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this documentation.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is the-- my official naturalization papers sent to the United States. That's when I became a citizen, and I changed my name from Sojbel to Sobel, Nathan Sobel. It was done in January 28, 1958. I just noticed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about these coins.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are not coins. These are medals. A medal is a-- these medals are-- present the [NON-ENGLISH], the, the, the Holocaust of the region of Poland where I come from. This represents a thousand-year history, a hundred communities, and, and six million Jews that died. And it tells all the shtetls inside, inside of a concave plate. And it goes in a-- in a spiral until you come to the center. You see a torch coming out. And it says the word, Wolyn, which is the region that I come from. And it has six candles, six lights, for the six million. This is the reverse. The obverse shows-- this is the one to the left, which is the bronze. And it shows the building which is the [NON-ENGLISH], which is part of the Yad Vashem in Israel. And below it, you see a-- the Eternal Jew pointing-- looking in one direction and pointing in another direction. He is looking away from it, from-- out of pain. And he points to the left to never forget and always remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the hand points upstairs. If you're religious, you say, God, how could you do it? And if you're not religious, you say, God, you're guilty. Why you-- why you let it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one to the right medal is the obverse and reverse again. That's a peace medal that I designed. It took me seven years to research the word 'peace' from-- as man as spoke it in the past 5,000 years. It has over a hundred languages, every word for peace. And to the left-- the medal to the left of the series, is Middle East peace, which I call Middle East Peace. And it has the facial, facial presentation of Abraham, patriarch Abraham. And out of his two eyes, two bodies-- come two facial expressions. One is Ishmael to the right, and Israel to the left-- is the two countries, two sons of Abraham, like two eyes in the head. The one to the right, I name Universal Peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this plaque.

speaker: interviewee
text: This plaque represents that I was honored with the planting of a thousand trees by AINA, American Israel Numismatic Association. I personally also planted a thousand trees in Israel in memory of my entire family that perished in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this manuscript.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a draft of a manuscript called the Shoahaggadah. It has nothing to do with Passover, but it has to do with the Holocaust, 27 day of Nisan. The Shoahaggadah, I conceived. And it has to do the job that the Haggadah does for the Exodus, so does the Shoahaggadah have to do for the Holocaust to remember. Every year people would read it in the synagogue or in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the '80s and the 1990s, I was director of urban renewal for New York City. And in my capacity, I created eight volumes of an urban renewal atlas of all the projects, 200 projects in New York City. OK. This is the NAHOS pamphlet I created last year. This is for the organization. And I've done other creations in addition to that.
